title: Cylindrical Trap Dependence in the Unitary Fermi Gas
abstract: The unitary Fermi gas serves as a tunable realization of a strongly coupled CFT, making it a powerful system for probing universal quantum many-body phenomena. Precise measurement of its properties remains experimentally challenging: finite-temperature effects and spatial inhomogeneity introduced by external trapping potentials can significantly distort observables. Cylindrical trap geometries are commonly used in experiments. This note extends an existing theoretical framework to this geometry, deriving how the cylindrical confinement modifies the dynamic structure factor at zero temperature. These results provide a necessary correction for the interpretation of experimental spectra in trapped unitary gases.
summary: This paper extends the theoretical description of a unitary Fermi gas from spherical to cylindrical trap geometries. It demonstrates how the interplay between longitudinal and transverse confinement modifies the dynamic structure factor. These results are crucial for accurately interpreting experimental measurements of density fluctuations in trapped quantum gases.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of interpreting experimental spectra in trapped unitary Fermi gases, specifically how cylindrical trap geometries modify the dynamic structure factor and phonon dispersion relation.
main_result: The study derives how cylindrical confinement modifies the dynamic structure factor, finding that longitudinal confinement contributes a convex correction while transverse confinement contributes a concave correction, with the net effect being convex for typical experimental parameters.
method: The authors use an Effective Field Theory (EFT) framework based on superfluid hydrodynamics, employing a Wentzel-Kramers-Brillouin (WKB) expansion and a hierarchy of scales approach to analyze different energy regimes.
model_or_system: A unitary Fermi gas (a strongly coupled, nonrelativistic Conformal Field Theory) realized using ultracold 6Li atoms, confined in a cylindrical geometry with power-law radial potentials and axial light sheets.
key_observables: Dynamic structure factor S(q, omega), phonon dispersion relation, and the energy spectrum.
important_parameters: Bertsch parameter (xi), speed of sound (cs), dispersion parameter (gamma), aspect ratio (epsilon), and low-energy constants (c0, c1, c2).
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the zero-temperature limit, the radial potential is assumed to approximate a hard-wall box, and the Bragg pulse is assumed to be in the linear response regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the condensate in cylindrical geometry; Figure 2 shows the energy spectrum and the accuracy of the WKB fit using numerical diagonalization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of trap-induced distortions, extends a spherical EFT framework to cylindrical geometry, identifies three physical energy regimes, derives the modified dynamic structure factor, and compares analytical WKB results with numerical diagonalization.
why_interesting: It provides essential corrections for interpreting Bragg spectroscopy data in ultracold atom experiments, specifically regarding how trap anisotropy breaks isotropy and modifies the phonon dispersion relation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 – Condensate confined in cylinder-shape geometry, via a power law potential in the transverse direction and by end caps in the longitudinal direction. There is no potential along z; the solutions are plane waves with frequency depending on the boundary condi- tions.
